frameworks.name: causal perturbation of sensory cortex
frameworks.one_line: Reversible focal inactivation to link MT activity to perceptual behavior

frameworks.name: perceptual decision-making
frameworks.one_line: Direction discrimination with concurrent confidence report

frameworks.name: downstream readout compensation
frameworks.one_line: Readout circuits adapt to inactivation over short and long timescales

frameworks.name: common mechanism for choice and confidence
frameworks.one_line: Choice and confidence reflect a shared decision computation

core_question: Does focal, reversible optogenetic suppression of direction-tuned MT neurons causally bias choice and confidence in a perceptual decision task?
key_assumptions: Inactivating MT neurons with consistent direction tuning probes a behaviorally relevant population
Hyperpolarizing opsins yield interpretable, fine-scale suppression in primate cortex
Choice and confidence reports share a common decision variable
manipulations: Randomly-interleaved trials with vs without optogenetic suppression in MT
Targeted neurons with consistent direction tuning
Varied visual stimulus duration including trials longer than ~350 ms
key_findings: Inactivation systematically biased direction choice and confidence
Behavioral effects attenuated over the course of each session
Effects largely vanished on trials with stimuli longer than ~350 ms
Choice and confidence showed similar trends under perturbation
interpretations: Downstream readout of MT compensates for the perturbation on both sub-second and tens-of-minutes timescales
Parallel effects on choice and confidence support a common underlying decision mechanism
